text: Thalassemia patients should avoid iron-rich foods like red meat, liver, shellfish, and spinach as excess iron accumulates in organs causing serious damage. Eat calcium-rich foods like dairy, broccoli, and almonds. Take folic acid supplements 5mg daily as prescribed. Stay hydrated with 2-3 litres of water daily.

Thalassemia major patients need regular blood transfusions every 2-4 weeks to maintain hemoglobin above 9-10 g/dL. Avoid strenuous exercise during low hemoglobin periods. Regular bone density checks are important as thalassemia weakens bones. Chelation therapy (deferoxamine or deferasirox) helps remove excess iron from organs.

Thalassemia patients cannot donate blood. They require blood transfusions themselves — often every 2-4 weeks for major thalassemia. Family members (siblings, parents) are strongly encouraged to donate regularly as compatible blood from related donors reduces antibody formation risks.

Sickle cell patients should drink plenty of water — at least 8-10 glasses daily to prevent sickling crisis. Eat folate-rich foods like leafy greens, beans, and lentils. Avoid alcohol, high-fat foods, and dehydrating drinks. Small frequent meals are better than large ones.

Avoid extreme temperatures, dehydration, and high altitude as these trigger sickling crises. Rest during pain crises and apply heat to painful areas. Take prescribed hydroxyurea as it reduces crisis frequency. Annual eye and kidney checkups are essential. Avoid high-impact sports; swimming and walking are ideal.

Hemophilia patients should avoid contact sports — football, wrestling, boxing. Use protective gear always and carry medical alert identification. Dental procedures need factor replacement beforehand. Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs as they worsen bleeding. Physiotherapy helps maintain joint health after bleeds.

Maintain healthy weight to reduce joint stress in hemophilia. Vitamin K from leafy greens may interfere with warfarin if prescribed — consult doctor before dietary changes. Stay physically active with low-impact exercise like swimming. High calcium diet supports bone health since joint bleeds can damage surrounding bone.

Eat iron-rich foods: red meat, chicken, fish, lentils, beans, tofu, spinach, fortified cereals. Pair with vitamin C (oranges, tomatoes, bell peppers) to boost iron absorption by up to 3x. Avoid tea, coffee, and milk with meals as they inhibit iron absorption. Cook in iron cookware to add dietary iron.

Iron deficiency anemia patients should not donate blood until hemoglobin normalises above 12.5 g/dL for women and 13 g/dL for men. Iron supplements take 3-6 months of treatment to fully restore stores. Consult your doctor before donating. Eating iron-rich foods and taking supplements as prescribed will help you become eligible.

To donate blood you must be 18-65 years old, weigh at least 45kg, have hemoglobin above 12.5 g/dL for women and 13.0 g/dL for men, and not have donated in the last 90 days. Avoid donating if you have fever, cold, recent surgery, tattoo in last 6 months, or are on antibiotics.

Before donating: eat a light iron-rich meal 3 hours before, drink 500ml water, avoid fatty foods. After donating: rest 10-15 minutes, drink juice, avoid heavy exercise for 24 hours, keep bandage on for 4-5 hours, eat iron-rich food for 2 days to replenish stores. You can donate whole blood every 90 days.

O- is the universal donor — can donate red cells to all blood types. AB+ is the universal recipient — can receive from all types. Always match blood type in transfusions when possible to avoid reactions. Rh-negative patients must receive Rh-negative blood to avoid sensitisation. Crossmatching is done before all transfusions.

Aplastic anemia patients must avoid infections — wash hands frequently, avoid crowds during treatment. Bone marrow transplant is the only cure for severe aplastic anemia. Immunosuppressive therapy (ATG + cyclosporine) is used when transplant is not possible. Regular blood counts every 1-2 weeks are essential.

Iron is an essential mineral the body uses to make hemoglobin (carries oxygen in red blood cells) and myoglobin (carries oxygen in muscles). Adult men need 8 mg/day; women aged 19-50 need 18 mg/day due to menstrual losses; pregnant women need 27 mg/day. Iron is stored as ferritin in the liver, spleen, and bone marrow. Serum ferritin is the best test for iron stores — normal range 12-300 ng/mL for adults.

Iron deficiency is the most common nutritional deficiency worldwide and the leading cause of anemia. Symptoms: fatigue, weakness, pale skin, chest pain, fast heartbeat, shortness of breath, headache, dizziness, cold hands/feet, brittle nails, and pica (craving non-food items like clay or ice). Confirmed by low serum ferritin (<12 ng/mL) and low hemoglobin. Treatment: oral iron 150-200 mg elemental iron daily for 3-6 months. Take on empty stomach with vitamin C for best absorption.

Two types of dietary iron: heme iron (from meat, poultry, fish) absorbed at 15-35%; non-heme iron (from plants, fortified foods) absorbed at 2-20%. Enhancers of iron absorption: vitamin C, meat/fish/poultry (MFP factor). Inhibitors: phytates (in grains, legumes), calcium (dairy), polyphenols (tea, coffee, wine), oxalates (spinach). For maximum absorption take iron supplements on empty stomach with orange juice, 1-2 hours before or after dairy.

Thalassemia patients face iron overload from both repeated blood transfusions (each unit adds ~200-250mg iron) AND increased intestinal iron absorption due to ineffective erythropoiesis. Iron accumulates in the heart causing arrhythmia, liver causing cirrhosis, and endocrine glands causing diabetes and hypogonadism. Marker: serum ferritin above 1000 ng/mL requires chelation. Treatment: deferoxamine (injected subcutaneously 8-12 hours daily) or deferasirox (oral, once daily). AVOID iron supplements and iron-fortified foods completely.

Sickle cell patients on chronic transfusion therapy develop secondary iron overload (transfusional hemosiderosis). Monitor serum ferritin every 3 months — chelation when ferritin exceeds 1000 ng/mL. Sickle cell patients NOT receiving transfusions typically have normal-to-low iron and should eat iron-containing foods normally. Note: hydroxyurea (common sickle cell drug) may slightly raise ferritin — discuss with haematologist whether this represents true iron overload.

Top iron food sources (mg per serving): beef liver 5mg/85g, cooked lentils 3.3mg/half cup, tofu 3.4mg/half cup, kidney beans 2mg/half cup, canned sardines 2.1mg/85g, boiled spinach 3.2mg/half cup, pumpkin seeds 2.3mg/28g, dark chocolate 3.4mg/40g, moringa (drumstick leaves) 7mg/100g — excellent Indian source. Fortified cereals provide up to 18mg/serving. Animal sources (heme iron) are 2-3x more bioavailable than plant sources.

Hemophilia patients with frequent joint bleeds and internal bleeding lose significant iron. Chronic blood loss causes iron deficiency anemia on top of hemophilia. Annual ferritin testing is recommended. After major bleeding episodes, iron-rich foods should be increased and supplementation considered under medical guidance. Important: aspirin and NSAIDs which worsen bleeding in hemophilia also impair iron absorption by damaging gut mucosa.

Folate (vitamin B9) is essential for DNA synthesis, cell division, and red blood cell formation. It prevents neural tube defects during pregnancy. Adults need 400 mcg DFE (dietary folate equivalents) daily; pregnant women need 600 mcg DFE. Folate is water-soluble and not stored well in the body — daily intake is important. Folic acid (synthetic form in supplements) is 85% bioavailable vs 50% for food folate.

Folic acid 5 mg daily is STANDARD CARE for sickle cell disease. Sickle cell patients destroy red blood cells at 6-8x normal rate — each destroyed cell requires folate for replacement. Without supplementation, megaloblastic anemia develops on top of sickle cell anemia, worsening hemoglobin levels. This is one of the most important daily supplements for sickle cell patients — never skip it. Even if diet is good, food folate alone cannot keep up with the demand from accelerated erythropoiesis.

Thalassemia patients have high red blood cell turnover from premature haemolysis, which rapidly depletes folate. Without supplementation, folate deficiency develops causing megaloblastic changes — large, immature red cells that are even less effective than thalassaemic cells. Folic acid 5 mg daily is standard for all thalassemia major and intermedia patients. This is usually prescribed alongside chelation therapy and transfusion schedule.

Top folate food sources (mcg DFE per serving): boiled beef liver 215mcg/85g, boiled spinach 131mcg/half cup, black-eyed peas 105mcg/half cup, cooked asparagus 134mcg/4 spears, avocado 59mcg/half, fortified rice 90mcg/half cup, orange juice 35mcg/180ml, moringa leaves 40mcg/100g. Cooking destroys up to 50% of food folate — eat vegetables lightly steamed or raw when possible. Daily folate from food: 1 bowl palak dal + 1 cup rice + orange juice nearly covers the adult requirement.

Iron deficiency anemia and folate deficiency anemia frequently co-exist in India, especially in women and children. Both cause fatigue, weakness, pale skin, and shortness of breath. Blood tests distinguish them: iron deficiency causes small red cells (microcytic, low MCV); folate deficiency causes large red cells (macrocytic, high MCV). Treatment requires both supplements simultaneously when both are deficient — iron alone is insufficient if folate is also low.

Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is essential for red blood cell formation, neurological function, and DNA synthesis. Adults need 2.4 mcg/day. B12 is found almost exclusively in animal products. Vegans and strict vegetarians are at high risk of B12 deficiency and must supplement with 500-1000 mcg B12 daily. B12 is stored in the liver for 3-5 years, so deficiency develops slowly but causes irreversible nerve damage if untreated.

Vitamin B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia — large, dysfunctional red blood cells that cannot carry oxygen efficiently. Symptoms overlap with iron deficiency: fatigue, weakness, pale skin. BUT B12 deficiency also causes neurological damage — tingling/numbness in hands and feet, balance problems, memory issues, and dementia if untreated long-term. Distinguish by blood test: B12/folate deficiency shows high MCV (>100 fL); iron deficiency shows low MCV. Treatment: B12 injections 1000 mcg monthly OR high-dose oral B12 1000 mcg daily.

Top B12 food sources (mcg per serving): beef liver 70.7mcg/85g, clams 84.1mcg/85g, canned tuna 2.5mcg/85g, beef 1.4mcg/85g, milk 1.2mcg/240ml, yoghurt 1.1mcg/240ml, egg 0.6mcg/large, nutritional yeast 8.3mcg/15g, fortified plant milk 1.2mcg/240ml. For vegetarians in India: dairy and eggs are critical sources. Strict vegans MUST supplement — B12 is essentially absent from plant foods (seaweed and fermented foods have unreliable amounts).

Thalassemia patients with high red blood cell turnover consume more B12 as the body attempts to produce replacement cells. Vegetarian thalassemia patients are at particular risk of B12 deficiency. Annual serum B12 testing is advisable — levels below 200 pg/mL require supplementation. B12 deficiency in thalassemia patients causes megaloblastic changes on top of thalassaemic red cell abnormalities, severely worsening anemia. B12 supplementation alongside folic acid ensures optimal cell production.

Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is a powerful antioxidant essential for iron absorption, collagen synthesis, immune function, and wound healing. Adults need 65-90 mg/day. Smokers need 35 mg extra. Found in citrus fruits, bell peppers, amla, guava, strawberries, broccoli, kiwi. Vitamin C is water-soluble and not stored — daily consumption is needed. It dramatically enhances non-heme iron absorption when taken together with iron-rich meals.

Vitamin C is the most powerful enhancer of non-heme iron absorption from plant foods. Consuming 100mg vitamin C with a meal increases iron absorption by 67%. Practical Indian tips: squeeze lemon juice on dal or lentils, eat amla (Indian gooseberry — 600mg vitamin C per 100g, highest of any food) alongside iron-rich meals, drink fresh orange juice with iron supplements, add tomatoes and bell peppers to iron-rich dishes. Two amla daily covers all vitamin C needs and maximises iron absorption.

Sickle cell disease generates high oxidative stress as sickle cells rupture, releasing hemoglobin that produces free radicals damaging blood vessels and organs. Vitamin C as antioxidant neutralises oxidative damage. Adequate dietary vitamin C may reduce vaso-occlusive pain episode frequency. However, high-dose supplements (>500mg) should be avoided in patients with transfusional iron overload as excess vitamin C can mobilise stored iron and cause cardiac complications.

Thalassemia patients with iron overload must be cautious with vitamin C supplements. Megadoses (>200mg supplemental) in iron-overloaded patients can release stored iron into circulation causing cardiac arrhythmias. Small amounts from whole foods (1-2 pieces fruit daily) are safe. During deferoxamine chelation therapy, 100mg vitamin C taken 30 minutes before the infusion enhances iron mobilisation and excretion — this specific use is medical and should only be done under haematologist supervision.

Top vitamin C food sources (mg per 100g): amla/Indian gooseberry 600mg, guava 228mg, yellow bell pepper 183mg, kiwi 93mg, broccoli 89mg, orange 53mg, papaya 62mg, strawberry 59mg, tomato 14mg, lemon 53mg. Amla is extraordinary — one medium amla (20g) provides 120mg vitamin C. Amla murabba, amla juice, and dried amla are excellent ways to consume it daily. Cooking destroys 25-50% of vitamin C; eat fruits raw.

Vitamin K is essential for blood clotting — it activates clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X in the coagulation cascade. Two main forms: K1 (phylloquinone) from leafy greens, K2 (menaquinone) from fermented foods and animal products. Adults need 90-120 mcg/day. Vitamin K also supports bone health by activating osteocalcin. Vitamin K is fat-soluble — eat leafy greens with some fat (oil/ghee) for best absorption.

Hemophilia A and B involve genetic defects in clotting factors VIII and IX respectively — neither is vitamin K-dependent. Vitamin K supplements DO NOT treat hemophilia. However, if a hemophilia patient is on warfarin for another condition, dietary vitamin K directly antagonises warfarin. Consistent vitamin K intake is critical — do not suddenly increase or decrease leafy green consumption as this destabilises INR control. Inform your haematologist of any major dietary changes.

Top vitamin K1 food sources (mcg per 100g): kale 817mcg, Swiss chard 830mcg, natto 1103mcg, spinach 483mcg, mustard greens 257mcg, collard greens 407mcg, broccoli 102mcg, Brussels sprouts 177mcg, green onions 207mcg, cabbage 76mcg. One cup raw spinach provides 145mcg, exceeding the adult daily requirement. For blood clotting support, include dark green leafy vegetables daily in your diet.

Calcium is the most abundant mineral in the body, essential for bone and tooth strength, muscle contraction, nerve transmission, and blood clotting (factor activation). Adults need 1000mg/day; women over 50 and adults over 70 need 1200mg/day. Calcium absorption requires vitamin D. Indian best sources: ragi (finger millet) 344mg/100g, paneer 480mg/100g, sesame seeds 975mg/100g, milk 300mg/240ml.

Thalassemia patients have high rates of osteoporosis and fractures due to: bone marrow expansion crowding bone tissue, iron overload destroying bone-forming osteoblasts, hypogonadism from endocrine iron damage, and reduced physical activity. Management: calcium 1000-1200mg daily + vitamin D3 800-1000 IU daily is essential from childhood. Weight-bearing exercise (walking, dancing) strengthens bones. DEXA bone density scans recommended from age 10. Avoid smoking — dramatically worsens bone loss.

Top calcium food sources for Indian diet (mg per serving): paneer 480mg/100g, sesame/til seeds 975mg/100g (use til chutney, tahini), ragi/finger millet 344mg/100g (best plant source in India — use ragi roti, ragi malt), milk 300mg/240ml, curd/yoghurt 300mg/240ml, tofu (calcium-set) 350mg/100g, sardines with bones 325mg/85g, moringa leaves 440mg/100g. Ragi and sesame are exceptionally rich — recommend to all patients who cannot afford dairy.

Calcium significantly inhibits iron absorption when consumed together. A 500mg calcium dose can reduce iron absorption by 30-60%. This is one of the most common reasons iron supplementation fails in Indian patients. Key rules: take iron supplements 2 hours away from dairy products, calcium supplements, or calcium-rich foods. Do NOT take iron tablets with milk or chai. This separation is especially important for women taking iron for menstrual anemia while also consuming dairy for bone health.

Zinc is essential for immune function, wound healing, DNA synthesis, cell division, and taste/smell. Adults need 8-11 mg/day. Zinc deficiency is highly prevalent in India, especially among vegetarians, because phytates in grains and legumes block absorption. Symptoms: frequent infections, delayed wound healing, hair loss, poor appetite, taste/smell dysfunction, growth failure in children, and impaired immune response.

Zinc deficiency affects 40-60% of sickle cell patients. Causes: increased urinary zinc loss from sickle nephropathy, accelerated cell turnover consuming zinc for DNA synthesis. Effects: worsens immune deficiency (patients are already functionally asplenic), delays growth and sexual maturation in children, may increase crisis frequency. Clinical trials: zinc sulphate 25mg three times daily reduces painful crisis frequency by ~50%. Take zinc with food to reduce nausea. Monitor — excess zinc (>40mg/day long-term) blocks copper absorption.

Thalassemia patients frequently show zinc deficiency, particularly those on deferoxamine chelation which also chelates zinc alongside iron. Low zinc impairs growth, delays puberty, and increases infection susceptibility in thalassemia children. Annual serum zinc monitoring is recommended. Supplementation dose: 15-25 mg elemental zinc daily, separated from chelation infusion timing. Zinc-deficient thalassemia children on supplementation show improved growth velocity.

Top zinc food sources (mg per 100g): oysters 78mg (richest), beef 8.7mg, pumpkin seeds 7.6mg, hemp seeds 9.9mg, cashews 5.4mg, lentils 3.3mg, chickpeas 3.4mg, oats 4mg, tofu 1.6mg. For vegetarians: soak legumes overnight and sprout them to reduce phytates by 50%, significantly improving zinc absorption. Include pumpkin seeds (kaddu ke beej) — cheap, available, and zinc-rich.

Copper is required for iron metabolism (ceruloplasmin converts iron to its absorbable form), red blood cell formation, antioxidant enzymes (superoxide dismutase), collagen synthesis, and energy production. Adults need 900 mcg/day. Copper deficiency causes anemia indistinguishable from iron deficiency that does NOT improve with iron supplements. Sources: shellfish, organ meats, nuts (cashews, almonds), seeds, dark chocolate, mushrooms, legumes.

Copper deficiency anemia is frequently misdiagnosed as iron deficiency anemia. Both present with low hemoglobin, fatigue, and pallor. Critical difference: copper-deficiency anemia does NOT respond to iron supplementation after 3 months. If your iron tablets are not working, ask for serum copper and ceruloplasmin testing. Common causes of copper deficiency in India: excessive zinc supplementation (>40mg/day zinc blocks copper absorption), malabsorption syndromes, extended parenteral nutrition. Treatment: oral copper sulphate under medical guidance.

Top copper food sources (mcg per serving): beef liver 12,400mcg/85g, oysters 4,850mcg/85g, dark chocolate 501mcg/28g, cashews 622mcg/28g, sunflower seeds 519mcg/28g, mushrooms 650mcg/half cup cooked, lentils 497mcg/half cup, almonds 290mcg/28g. One handful of cashews daily provides adequate copper. Note: cooking in copper vessels (as traditionally done in some Indian households) adds trace copper to food.

Vitamin D is essential for calcium absorption (increases by 30-40%), bone mineralisation, immune function, and muscle strength. The body synthesises vitamin D from sunlight (UVB) on skin — 15-20 minutes midday sun on arms and legs produces 1000-2000 IU. Adults need 600-800 IU/day from diet or supplements. Despite abundant sunlight, 70-80% of Indians are vitamin D deficient due to indoor work, clothing coverage, and dark skin reducing UVB penetration.

Thalassemia patients have very high rates of vitamin D deficiency which severely worsens their bone disease. Without adequate vitamin D, calcium cannot be absorbed efficiently, accelerating osteoporosis. Annual serum 25-OH vitamin D testing is recommended — levels below 20 ng/mL require treatment. Standard supplementation: vitamin D3 1000-2000 IU daily. In cases of severe deficiency (<10 ng/mL), loading doses under medical supervision may be needed. Combined calcium + vitamin D supplementation is the minimum standard of care.

Vitamin D deficiency is highly prevalent in sickle cell disease (40-80% of patients). Low vitamin D is strongly associated with more frequent vaso-occlusive pain crises, higher rates of acute chest syndrome, and increased susceptibility to infections. Clinical trials: supplementation with 4000 IU vitamin D3 daily reduces pain episode frequency and improves quality of life. All sickle cell patients should have vitamin D checked and supplemented to maintain levels above 30 ng/mL.

Top vitamin D food sources (IU per serving): salmon 570 IU/85g, swordfish 566 IU/85g, canned tuna 268 IU/85g, sardines 193 IU/85g, UV-mushrooms 400 IU/half cup, fortified cow milk 120 IU/240ml, egg yolk 41 IU each. Note: food alone rarely provides sufficient vitamin D for Indian patients. Spend 15-20 minutes in morning/afternoon sun daily (not midday in summer) with arms exposed. Vitamin D3 supplements (1000-2000 IU) are safe and inexpensive for daily use.

Magnesium reduces red cell dehydration in sickle cell disease by modulating potassium-chloride cotransporter activity, keeping red cells hydrated and less prone to sickling. Clinical studies show oral magnesium pidolate 540 mg twice daily reduces the proportion of dense sickled cells. Adults need 310-420 mg magnesium daily. Food sources: pumpkin seeds 156mg/28g, chia seeds 111mg/28g, almonds 80mg/28g, spinach 78mg/half cup, black beans 60mg/half cup, dark chocolate 50mg/28g, banana 32mg.

Thalassemia patients on chelation therapy and with endocrine complications may have low magnesium levels. Hypomagnesemia contributes to muscle cramps, cardiac arrhythmias, and worsens bone disease. Magnesium-rich foods or supplements (200-400 mg daily) help prevent these complications. Magnesium also reduces the oxidative stress associated with chronic hemolysis. Best food sources: nuts, seeds, dark leafy greens, legumes, and whole grains.

Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) have potent anti-inflammatory effects directly relevant to sickle cell disease. Sickle cell patients have chronically elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, IL-6) and increased platelet aggregation increasing vaso-occlusion risk. Clinical trials of omega-3 supplementation (500mg-3g EPA+DHA daily) show reduced pain episodes and improved red cell membrane flexibility. Include fatty fish 2-3x/week: sardines (cheapest), mackerel, salmon. For vegetarians: flaxseed oil (ALA omega-3) 1 tablespoon daily.

Thalassemia patients face elevated cardiovascular risk from chronic anemia, iron deposition in the heart, and oxidative stress. Omega-3 fatty acids reduce triglycerides (often elevated in thalassemia), inflammation, and improve endothelial function protecting the heart. Fish oil 1-2g EPA+DHA daily is a reasonable supplement for thalassemia patients with cardiac risk. For vegetarians: 1 tablespoon ground flaxseed (alsi) daily in roti atta or smoothie is a practical source of ALA omega-3.

Dehydration is the single biggest trigger of sickle cell crisis — even mild dehydration (1-2% body weight) concentrates red cells, promoting sickling. Drink 8-10 glasses (2-2.5 litres) daily — more in heat, illness, or exercise. Carry water everywhere. During fever or diarrhoea use ORS (oral rehydration solution) which is better than plain water for replenishing electrolytes. Avoid alcohol, coffee, and cola which dehydrate. Set phone reminders to drink water every hour if you forget.

Blood donors should drink an extra 500ml water in the hours before donation. Good hydration makes veins prominent and easier to access, reduces lightheadedness post-donation, and speeds plasma volume recovery. After donation, drink 2-3 extra glasses of water or fruit juice over the following 24 hours to replenish plasma volume lost (approximately 450ml). Avoid alcohol for 24 hours post-donation as it causes further dehydration and vasodilation.

Ideal pre-donation meal (2-3 hours before): include iron-rich protein (eggs, dal, chicken), avoid fatty foods (burgers, fries, samosas) as fat makes blood lipemic and potentially unusable. Include vitamin C to support iron stores. Good Indian options: poha with peas, idli sambhar, dal rice with lemon, egg on toast with tomato. Avoid heavy meals (causes vasovagal reactions) or fasting (causes low blood sugar and dizziness).

An anti-inflammatory diet reduces oxidative stress in all blood disorders. Principles: increase colourful fruits and vegetables (antioxidants — polyphenols, carotenoids), choose whole grains over refined (more folate, zinc, iron), include omega-3 rich foods (fish, flaxseed, walnuts), use turmeric (curcumin is anti-inflammatory) and ginger daily in cooking, reduce processed foods, refined sugar, and trans fats. This dietary pattern improves inflammation markers and quality of life in thalassemia, sickle cell disease, and hemophilia patients.

Best Indian foods for blood disorder patients: ragi (finger millet) — rich in calcium AND iron, use for rotis; moringa/drumstick leaves — highest iron (7mg/100g), folate, calcium, vitamin C of any Indian vegetable, add to sambhar, dal, or eat as dry sabji; amla — highest vitamin C (600mg/100g), eat 2 daily; til (sesame) — extremely high calcium, use in chutney; methi (fenugreek) seeds — soak overnight, eat in curries for iron; dark jaggery — contains iron, substitute for sugar. Avoid tea/coffee within 1 hour of iron-rich meals.

Hemolysis (premature red blood cell destruction) in sickle cell and thalassemia depletes: folate (for replacement cell production), zinc and magnesium (lost in urine), and antioxidants (damaged by free radical release). All haemolytic anemia patients benefit from: folic acid 5mg daily (non-negotiable), B12 monitoring (annual), zinc supplementation 15-25mg daily, vitamin E 400 IU as antioxidant, and vitamin D for bone protection. Avoid iron supplements unless serum ferritin is confirmed low.

During chelation therapy for iron overload: deferoxamine works best on empty stomach. 100mg vitamin C 30 min before infusion enhances iron excretion (medical supervision only). Zinc 15-25mg daily is needed as deferoxamine also chelates zinc. Calcium + vitamin D protect bones. Magnesium-rich foods reduce muscle cramps from the infusion. Maintain 2+ litres daily fluid intake to help kidneys excrete iron-chelate complexes. Deferasirox (oral) should be taken on empty stomach with water or juice — NOT with aluminium antacids.

During sickle cell pain crisis: prioritise hydration — 200-250ml water every hour. Warm fluids better than cold (cold triggers vasospasm). Continue folic acid and vitamin D supplements even during crisis. Eat easy-to-digest foods if appetite poor: khichdi, soup, dal water, banana. Do not skip meals — nutrition is needed for healing. Avoid caffeine and alcohol. After crisis, focus on anti-inflammatory foods: turmeric milk, fish, omega-3 rich foods, and extra hydration for 1-2 weeks.
